title: What is hallucination and why does it happen?

questions.question: Why do LLMs hallucinate?
options: Because they are connected to unreliable data sources
Because they optimise for statistically plausible text, not factual accuracy
Because they run out of memory during long responses
Because engineers haven't programmed enough rules to catch errors
questions.explanation: Hallucination is a direct consequence of next-token prediction. The model is optimising for 'what sounds right next', not 'what is true'. It has no truth-checking mechanism built in.

questions.question: What makes hallucination especially dangerous compared to a model simply saying 'I don't know'?
options: Hallucinations are harder to detect because they are delivered with confidence
Hallucinations always contain more words than a truthful answer
Hallucinations only occur in technical domains like medicine and law
Hallucinations cause the model to slow down noticeably
questions.explanation: Hallucinated content often reads as more authoritative than correct content, because the model is generating what a confident, helpful answer looks like. The confidence is the problem, it bypasses the reader's critical instinct.

questions.question: Karel tells a fraud victim: 'Your report has been filed and your card has been frozen.' The customer relaxes and waits. Four days later they discover nothing was done. What type of hallucination is this?
options: Factual hallucination; Karel stated an incorrect fact
Citation hallucination; Karel invented a reference that doesn't exist
Action hallucination; Karel described an action as completed when it wasn't performed
Reasoning hallucination; Karel followed a flawed chain of logic
questions.explanation: This is one of the most dangerous failure modes in agentic AI: the model generates text describing a completed action without actually calling the tool. In a chatbot, hallucinating a fact is annoying. In an agent, hallucinating an action has real consequences.
